title: Conflict Signature

introduction.subtitle: What do you actually do when disagreement becomes real? Five approaches and your signature across them.
introduction.description:
Most people use one conflict reflex more than the others — and that reflex is invisible to them until it costs something. This maps yours. Each approach has a context where it is the right tool and one where it fails.

Mark your first and second instinct. If you are split, pick what you do when the stakes are real.

Use the result as language for reflection and conversation, not as a permanent label. The terms are meant to make patterns easier to notice, compare, and discuss.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is your choice.
introduction.disclaimer: Original instrument. This is not a validated psychometric assessment.

questions.text: A co-founder is making decisions without you. What is your first instinct?

options.text: Name it directly and demand a real conversation.
Offer a trade: I won’t escalate if you bring me in from now on.
Let it pass for now to preserve the relationship.
Stay busy elsewhere and let it settle.
Reframe it as a shared problem about how we decide together.

conceptHelp.title: Conflict response: A co-founder is making decisions without you. What is your first instinct
conceptHelp.summary: A conflict response is the first strategy you tend to use when tension appears: confront, negotiate, accommodate, avoid, or transform the disagreement into a new shared frame. This question tests that pattern in "A co-founder is making decisions without you. What is your first instinct?".
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Conflict style matters because the same instinct can be useful in one situation and costly in another. Naming the default makes it easier to choose deliberately.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Answer with your likely first move under pressure, before you have time to craft the mature version of yourself.
conceptHelp.example: The options contrast Name it directly and demand a real conversation. / Offer a trade: I won’t escalate if you bring me in from now on. / Let it pass for now to preserve the relationship.; your answer shows the conflict move you would probably trust first.

questions.text: A colleague takes credit for your work in a meeting. You...

options.text: Say, plainly, that the work was mine.
Propose how we will credit contributions going forward.
Let it go this once; raise it privately later.
Move on; the work speaks for itself eventually.
Bridge to a joint framing that serves the project, then raise credit privately.

questions.text: Your team splits over a direction. You...

options.text: Push for the decision in the open.
Find the middle that most can live with.
Back the option most others want.
Defer the decision until it resolves itself.
Reframe the disagreement so both sides win part of it.

questions.text: A customer publicly attacks your venture. You...

options.text: Respond directly and contest the claim.
Offer a concrete remedy and ask them to update publicly.
Apologize publicly to end the conflict.
Decline to engage publicly.
Treat it as a discovery moment about a real unmet need.

questions.text: A teammate repeatedly misses commitments. You...

options.text: Address the pattern directly and set consequences.
Propose a revised commitment and check-in cadence.
Cover for them to keep the work moving.
Hope the pattern corrects itself.
Ask what is making the commitments impossible for them.

questions.text: Someone you respect criticizes your work harshly. You...

options.text: Push back on the specific criticisms.
Accept the valid points and ask for the rest to be reframed.
Absorb it; they probably mean well.
Thank them and set it aside.
Treat the critique as data about both the work and the lens.

questions.text: Two allies in your network are in conflict. You...

options.text: Take a side and defend it.
Broker a compromise between them.
Mediate by yielding your own position.
Decline to be involved.
Help them see the shared project both want defended.

questions.text: A rule you find unjust is imposed on your venture. You...

options.text: Contest it openly.
Seek an exception or phased application.
Comply and continue.
Work around it quietly.
Reframe it as a question for the body that made the rule, and propose a better one.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
